title: Gen Z’s Relationship With AI
text:
Leadership
Careers
Gen Z’s Relationship With AI
By
Tarun Galagali
,
Contributor.
Forbes contributors publish independent expert analyses and insights.
Tarun Galagali explores AI and building high-performance cultures.
Follow Author
Aug 25, 2025, 12:34pm EDT
Last month, Gallup surveyed 3,465 Gen Z workers about their AI usage and found a startling contradiction: while
nearly half (47%) use generative AI weekly
, 41% feel anxious about the technology. These are supposed to be the "AI natives"—the generation that grew up with smartphones and should naturally adapt to artificial intelligence. Yet they're struggling with AI literacy in ways that could define their entire careers.
This disconnect reveals something crucial about AI's impact on the youngest workforce: being comfortable with technology doesn't automatically translate to being competent with AI. And the implications extend far beyond individual career prospects—they could reshape how entire industries approach training, hiring, and workplace culture.
AI's Impact on Generation Z Is Important for Workplaces To Understand
getty
The Overconfidence Problem
EY's recent survey of Gen Z workers reveals a troubling pattern: young professionals
overestimate their AI literacy
while underperforming on actual AI tasks. Even those who rated themselves as "very knowledgeable" scored poorly when writing prompts or evaluating AI shortfalls. The confidence gap is dangerous—it creates workers who think they understand AI while lacking the critical evaluation skills to use it effectively.
This overconfidence manifests in workplace behavior.
Research from Aithor
found that almost 80% of Gen Z professionals use AI tools for over half their work tasks—a staggering level of dependence for a generation just entering the workforce. More concerning, 38% report being dependent on AI to complete daily tasks, yet many lack the skills to evaluate when AI outputs are wrong or inappropriate.
"Gen Z are the world leaders of tomorrow and some of today's leading technology users," notes Naria Santa Lucia from Microsoft Philanthropies. "With more exposure to AI at the start of their career than any other generation, they're also the most exposed to the impacts of AI. Educators and employers must prioritize skilling this generation in AI literacy."
MORE FOR YOU
The Skills Development Paradox
While Gen Z embraces AI enthusiastically,
research shows concerning gaps
in fundamental workplace skills. The TalentLMS study found that heavy AI users struggle more with teamwork, communication, and building workplace relationships. This creates a paradox: the tools meant to enhance productivity may be undermining the soft skills that drive long-term career success.
The data reveals specific areas of concern:
63% of Gen Z workers believe AI integration should be accompanied by soft skills training
Workers report AI negatively affecting their ability to collaborate and communicate
Heavy AI users show decreased problem-solving and critical-thinking development
Yet Gen Z recognizes the importance of these skills.
Udemy's research
found that while 30% want to build AI skills, they equally prioritize communication (30%) and critical thinking (26%). Most telling: 84% consider soft skills critical to professional success.
The challenge is integration. How do you develop human capabilities while leveraging AI tools?
The Entry-Level Job Crisis
AI is fundamentally disrupting how Gen Z enters the workforce. Traditional entry-level roles—historically the training ground for new workers—are increasingly automated away.
LinkedIn data shows
a 14% increase in applications per entry-level role, as AI eliminates routine tasks that once provided learning opportunities.
The impact is already visible: projected declines in traditional entry-level positions in food services, customer service, sales, and office support could account for nearly 84% of occupational shifts by 2030. Meanwhile, employers are placing new demands on candidates—two-thirds of managers say they wouldn't hire someone without AI skills, and 71% prefer less experienced candidates with AI skills over more experienced ones without them.
This creates a catch-22 for Gen Z: they need AI skills to get hired, but traditional pathways to developing workplace competence are disappearing. The solution isn't just AI training—it's redesigning how young workers develop professional judgment and expertise.
The Educational Disconnect
Perhaps most troubling is the gap between educational and workplace AI expectations.
EY's research found
that while only 7% of Gen Z expect to be discouraged from using AI in the workplace, this jumps to 21% in educational settings. This disconnect means students aren't getting consistent preparation for an AI-powered workforce.
The educational approach matters because it shapes fundamental attitudes about AI use. When schools discourage AI while employers encourage it, students develop conflicting mental models about appropriate use cases, ethical boundaries, and evaluation criteria.
Meanwhile,
Salesforce data shows
that 70% of Gen Z report using AI tools, and 52% trust the technology to help them make informed decisions. But trust without literacy is dangerous—it leads to over-reliance without critical evaluation.
What This Means for Organizations
The implications extend beyond individual career development. Organizations hiring Gen Z workers face a generation that's simultaneously the most AI-experienced and the most vulnerable to AI-related blind spots.
Smart companies are adapting their approach:
1. Redesigning entry-level roles
: Instead of eliminating these positions, forward-thinking employers are refocusing them on higher-value activities requiring critical thinking and creativity—areas where humans outperform machines.
2. Implementing AI literacy programs
: Beyond technical training, these programs teach prompt engineering, output evaluation, and appropriate use cases. The goal isn't just AI competence but AI wisdom.
3. Emphasizing human-AI collaboration
: Rather than viewing AI as a replacement for human skills, successful programs teach complementary capabilities—using AI to handle routine tasks while developing human judgment for complex decisions.
4. Measuring the right outcomes
: Organizations tracking both productivity metrics and skill development are better positioned to identify when AI use enhances versus undermines long-term capability building.
The Path Forward
Gen Z's relationship with AI will define the next decade of workplace transformation. The key insight is that digital nativity doesn't equal AI readiness. Being comfortable with technology is different from understanding its boundaries, biases, and appropriate applications.
The most successful Gen Z workers will be those who develop what researchers call "AI wisdom"—not just technical proficiency but the judgment to know when to trust AI outputs, when to override them, and when to avoid AI entirely.
For employers, the opportunity is clear: invest in comprehensive AI literacy that goes beyond tool training to include critical thinking, ethical evaluation, and human skill development. The organizations that get this balance right will have a significant advantage in attracting and developing the most capable young talent.
The AI revolution isn't just about technology—it's about developing human capabilities alongside artificial ones. Gen Z will either become the most effective human-AI collaborators in history, or a generation overly dependent on tools they don't fully understand.
The choice is being made right now, in classrooms and offices across the country. The stakes couldn't be higher.
I write about the intersection of AI and performance management for Forbes. I'm the founder of
Mandala
, an AI Coaching Platform for Managers.
